items.title: Corneal ulcer with stromal infiltrate
items.contentHtml:
Corneal ulcer with stromal infiltrate

  • Stromal infiltrate is the accumulation of polymorphonuclear cells in the corneal stroma, characteristic of bacterial keratitis.
  • The corneal ulcer is defined by loss of epithelium with underlying infiltrate; fluorescein staining demarcates the area of absent epithelium.
  • The stromal infiltrate can progress to corneal thinning and corneal perforation if untreated.
  • Clinically it presents with pain, photophobia, and an epithelial defect visible with a slit lamp.
  • The Gram stain and culture corneal are the standard for identifying the causal agent.

items.title: Pseudomonas aeruginosa in contact lenses
items.contentHtml:
Pseudomonas aeruginosa in contact lenses greenish.
  • Risk factors: nighttime use, storage in tap water, and poor disinfection of lenses.
  • The empirical treatment of choice is topical ciprofloxacin or a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin, gatifloxacin).
  • The Gram stain shows gram-negative bacilli; culture on blood agar and MacConkey confirms the diagnosis.

items.title: Staphylococcus aureus most common cause
items.contentHtml:
Staphylococcus aureus most common cause

  • Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of bacterial keratitis in the general population, especially after corneal trauma.
  • It produces a corneal ulcer with well-circumscribed stromal infiltrate and yellowish purulent discharge.
  • The Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters; culture on blood agar confirms the diagnosis.
  • Initial empirical treatment includes topical ciprofloxacin or topical vancomycin if there is resistance.
  • It can be complicated by hypopion and corneal perforation in severe cases.

items.title: Gram stain and corneal culture
items.contentHtml:
Gram stain and corneal culture

  • The Gram stain and corneal culture are the diagnostic standard in bacterial keratitis; They are obtained by corneal scraping with a Kimura spatula.
  • The Gram stain allows the initial identification of the agent (gram-positive cocci such as Staphylococcus aureus or gram-negative bacilli such as Pseudomonas aeruginosa).
  • The corneal culture is performed on blood agar (for gram-positive), chocolate agar (for Neisseria), and MacConkey agar (for gram-negative).
  • It is recommended to take samples before starting antibiotics, although empirical treatment should not be delayed.
  • The sensitivity of the culture is >80% if done correctly.

items.title: Chymosis and purulent discharge
items.contentHtml:
Chimosis and purulent discharge

  • Chimosis is edema of the bulbar conjunctiva, which in bacterial keratitis indicates intense inflammation and possible extension to the conjunctiva.
  • purulent discharge is characteristic of infections by Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa; its color can guide the agent (yellowish in gram-positive, greenish in Pseudomonas aeruginosa).
  • The presence of chymosis and purulent discharge together with corneal ulcer suggests severe bacterial keratitis and risk of corneal perforation.
  • Intensive topical treatment with ciprofloxacin topical every 15-30 minutes is the first line.

items.title: Fluorescein stains ulcer green
items.contentHtml:
Fluorescein stains ulcer green

  • Topical fluorescein stains the area of corneal ulcer where epithelium is missing, green, under cobalt blue light.
  • The staining pattern helps differentiate bacterial keratitis (epithelial defect with irregular borders) from herpetic keratitis (dendritic pattern).
  • Fluorescein does not stain the stromal infiltrate directly; it only demarcates epithelial loss.
  • An epithelial defect that persists after 24-48 hours of treatment suggests resistance or poor adherence.
  • fluorescein is contraindicated if perforation is suspected, as it can stain the aqueous humor (Seidel's sign).

items.title: Topical ciprofloxacin is empirical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Topical ciprofloxacin is an empirical treatment

  • Topical ciprofloxacin is the empirical treatment of choice in bacterial keratitis due to its broad spectrum against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
  • Initial dose: 1 drop every 15-30 minutes for the first 6-12 hours, then reduce according to response
  • Alternatives: topical moxifloxacin or gatifloxacin (fourth generation fluoroquinolones) with better stromal penetration.
  • In severe cases with hypopion or corneal perforation, fortified: cefazolin 50 mg/mL + topical gentamicin 14 mg/mL is added.
  • Corticoids are Contraindicated without concomitant antibiotics due to the risk of infectious exacerbation.

items.title: Hypopion in severe keratitis
items.contentHtml:
Hypopion in severe keratitis

  • Hypopion is the accumulation of leukocytes in the anterior chamber, visible as a white-yellowish level; indicates severe bacterial keratitis with an intense inflammatory response.
  • Its presence is associated with extensive corneal ulcer, deep stromal infiltrate, and high risk of corneal perforation.
  • Management requires intensive topical antibiotic therapy (e.g. topical ciprofloxacin every 15-30 min) and daily evaluation with a lamp. cleft.
  • The hypopion is not specific for bacteria; It can be seen in fungal or herpetic keratitis, so culture is required to confirm.
  • Gram stain and corneal culture are mandatory for hypopion.

items.title: Risk factors: trauma and lenses
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors: trauma and lenses

  • The main risk factors for bacterial keratitis are corneal trauma (abrasions, foreign bodies) and use of contact lenses, especially prolonged use or with inadequate hygiene.
  • Other factors: previous ocular surgery, ocular surface disease (keratoconjunctivitis sicca, blepharitis), immunosuppression and use of topical corticosteroids.
  • In contact lens users, the main agent is Pseudomonas aeruginosa; in trauma, Staphylococcus aureus and streptococcus.
  • Prevention includes education on lens hygiene and avoiding sleeping with them.

items.title: Contraindicated corticosteroids without antibiotics
items.contentHtml:
Corticosteroids contraindicated without antibiotics

  • Corticosteroids are contraindicated without antibiotics in bacterial keratitis because they suppress the immune response, allowing bacterial proliferation and increasing the risk of corneal perforation.
  • Their use is only considered after 24-48 hours of effective antibiotic therapy and with a negative culture or favorable clinical response.
  • In herpetic keratitis, corticosteroids are absolutely contraindicated without concomitant antivirals; differential diagnosis with herpetic keratitis is crucial.
  • fluorescein helps in DDx: dendritic pattern suggests herpes, while geographic defect with stromal infiltrate suggests bacterial.

items.title: Corneal perforation as a complication
items.contentHtml:
Corneal perforation as a complication

  • Corneal perforation is a serious complication of untreated bacterial keratitis or with deep stromal infiltrate; It manifests with a flat anterior chamber and a positive Seidel sign (aqueous humor leak with fluorescein).
  • Risk factors: hypopion, extensive corneal ulcer, use of corticosteroids without antibiotics, and delay in treatment.
  • Management includes intensive topical antibiotic therapy, and if the perforation is <2 mm, glue can be used. tissue or therapeutic contact lens; if larger, penetrating keratoplasty is required.
  • Gram stain and corneal culture are essential to guide therapy.

items.title: Differential diagnosis with herpetic keratitis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with herpetic keratitis

  • The differential diagnosis with herpetic keratitis is based on the staining pattern with fluorescein: herpetic keratitis typically shows a dendritic ulcer (branched with terminal bulbs), while the bacterial one presents a geographic epithelial defect with infiltrate stromal.
  • Herpes keratitis usually has less purulent secretion and chymosis than severe bacterial keratitis.
  • The Gram stain and corneal culture are negative in herpes; PCR for HSV-1 is the diagnostic standard.
  • Herpes treatment is topical antiviral (acyclovir 3%) or systemic; corticosteroids are contraindicated without antibiotics and without antivirals in herpes.
